script:
Hello, I am Josh Weinberg — an AI-generated voice — and this is The Portfolio Podcast, an automated market recap. This is not financial advice. Friday, as of July 3 2026 at 12:33 AM ET — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio closed up one point two eight percent on the session, a gain of one thousand two hundred eighty-three dollars and fifty-four cents, bringing our total value to one hundred one thousand two hundred eighty-three dollars and fifty-four cents. A green Friday heading into the holiday weekend.

Now — Apple. Up six point six six percent in a single session. Three hundred eight dollars and sixty-three cents at the close. That is nearly a twenty-dollar single-day move on one of the most widely held stocks on the planet, and it just became the single biggest story in this portfolio today. What is driving it? A memory chip breakthrough. And the implications are enormous.

No macro news was available for Friday's session, so the story was written entirely at the stock level. Technology and communication services led the tape, with heavyweights like Apple and Microsoft posting outsized gains that likely gave the Nasdaq a meaningful lift on the day. Consumer discretionary was a split story — Amazon pushing higher while Tesla got absolutely hammered. Semiconductors were under pressure broadly, with AI-linked chip names seeing notable selling as fears surfaced about whether the blistering pace of AI-driven demand can actually hold.

Let's go deep on Apple, because this one deserves it. Apple closed at three hundred eight dollars and sixty-three cents, up nineteen dollars and twenty-seven cents, a gain of six point six six percent. That is a massive single-session move for a company sitting at the very top of the QQQM weight ladder at eight point nine seven percent of this portfolio. So what happened? According to reporting from Yahoo Finance, Apple may have found a solution to its memory problem — and that framing is key. The memory chip shortage driven by the artificial intelligence boom has been a real constraint across the tech sector. A Bloomberg report flagged that the semiconductor industry itself is urging the Trump administration not to intervene in ways that could distort the memory market further, warning that government meddling would worsen an already historic supply squeeze. Into that backdrop, the suggestion that Apple has found a path through — potentially a proprietary or alternative memory architecture — landed like a thunderclap. Separately, Investor's Business Daily flagged that Apple flashed a technical buy signal on Friday, adding fuel to the momentum. With Apple carrying the largest single weight in this portfolio, a six-plus-percent day on that name alone is a portfolio-level event. Watch for any formal product or supply chain announcement from Apple in the coming days — if there is substance behind this memory story, the follow-through could be significant.

Turning to Microsoft, which had itself a very solid Friday. Microsoft closed at three hundred ninety dollars and forty-nine cents, up seventeen dollars and forty-seven cents, a gain of four point six eight percent. That is the second-largest weighting in the portfolio at eight point four two percent, and a nearly five-percent gain is nothing to gloss over. The news flow around Microsoft was genuinely interesting on Friday. Kyndryl, the IT infrastructure spinoff, jumped over three percent after announcing an expanded partnership with Microsoft around its Sovereign Cloud platform — helping governments and regulated industries build cloud architectures that meet strict data residency requirements. That is a real and growing market, particularly in Europe and Asia, and it highlights Microsoft's expanding role as infrastructure for regulated AI. Meanwhile, Genpact launched an AI agent-powered platform built on Microsoft technology, targeting consumer goods companies. The cloud and enterprise AI story for Microsoft is very much alive and adding partners at a rapid clip.

Over at NVIDIA, Friday was a different kind of session. NVIDIA closed at one hundred ninety-four dollars and eighty-three cents, down five dollars and twenty-six cents, a loss of two point six three percent. That pullback came in the context of a broader AI chip sentiment wobble — Dell fell more than eight percent on Friday amid fears that AI-driven chip demand may be cooling, and that pressure bled into NVIDIA as well. NVIDIA sits at eight point one five percent of this portfolio, so the drag was felt. No direct company-specific news drove the move — this was macro sector pressure. The demand cooling narrative is worth monitoring closely heading into next week.

Alphabet closed at three hundred fifty-nine dollars and ninety-one cents, up two dollars and fifty-four cents, a gain of zero point seven one percent. A quiet but positive session for the Alphabet story. The notable item from Friday was a deep-dive piece on Alphabet's dividend history and future prospects, highlighting that the company has generated more than one hundred billion dollars in annual profit in recent years and now carries a market capitalization exceeding four point three trillion dollars as of mid twenty twenty-six. Not a market-moving headline, but a useful reminder of the fundamental depth sitting behind this position, which represents eight point zero one percent of the portfolio.

Meanwhile, Amazon had a genuinely solid Friday. Amazon closed at two hundred forty-two dollars and sixty-seven cents, up four dollars and thirty-three cents, a gain of one point eight two percent. Amazon's cloud business came into focus in an interesting way — Yahoo Finance ran a piece asking whether Amazon is still a buy given that Meta is reportedly planning to launch its own cloud business to sell AI computing power. That is a real competitive question for Amazon Web Services, which remains among the most profitable businesses on the planet. For now, the market voted with a green close. Amazon represents five point five two percent of this portfolio.

Broadcom did not have a good Friday. Broadcom closed at three hundred sixty dollars and forty-five cents, down seventeen dollars and thirty cents, a loss of four point five eight percent. No company-specific news directly drove that move, but the semiconductor sector pressure that hit NVIDIA clearly spread here as well. Broadcom sits at four point six one percent of the portfolio, and a nearly five-percent down day stings. The broader AI chip demand narrative is the key variable to watch for Broadcom heading into next week.

Meta had a strong session. Meta closed at five hundred eighty-two dollars and ninety cents, up nineteen dollars and sixty-one cents, a gain of three point four eight percent. The big headline swirling around Meta on Friday was the report that the company is planning to launch a cloud business to sell AI computing power — a direct challenge to Amazon Web Services and Microsoft Azure. That is an enormously ambitious move, and the market clearly liked it. If Meta executes on a cloud platform, it reshapes the competitive landscape in a major way. Meta represents four point four eight percent of the portfolio, and Friday's move added real value.

Tesla had an absolutely brutal Friday. Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-three dollars and forty-five cents, down twenty-seven dollars and fifteen cents, a loss of six point four six percent. That is a punishing single-session drop. Tesla's decline appears connected to the broader AI and tech growth stock selloff that hit names like Dell and others tied to high-momentum AI narratives. Tesla represents three point five four percent of the portfolio, so the damage, while real, was somewhat contained by its relatively smaller weighting. Still — a nearly seven-percent down day demands attention, and the stock will need to find support heading into next week.

Finally, Costco — a quiet, steady closer to round out the holdings. Costco closed at nine hundred fifty-one dollars and sixty-seven cents, up sixteen dollars and twenty cents, a gain of one point seven three percent. No major news directly tied to Costco on Friday, but the consumer staples sector showed resilience even as parts of the market sold off. At two point eight one percent of the portfolio, Costco is the smallest position in this top-ten rundown, but its stability on a mixed tape is exactly what you want from a defensive anchor name.

Three things to watch in the next twenty-four to forty-eight hours. First — any formal announcement from Apple regarding its memory chip solution. Given the magnitude of Friday's move, the market will be watching for substance behind that story when trading resumes after the holiday. Second — NVIDIA and the AI chip demand narrative. Watch for any analyst commentary or supply chain data that either confirms or refutes the cooling demand fear that knocked semiconductor names on Friday. Third — Meta's cloud ambitions. Any additional reporting or official confirmation from Meta about its planned cloud platform could move not just Meta but Amazon and Microsoft meaningfully when markets reopen next week.

That is your Friday, July 3rd recap. A strong day for the portfolio, lifted by Apple and Microsoft, with Tesla and Broadcom providing the friction. The next episode of The Portfolio Podcast drops at four forty-five PM ET on the next trading day. Keep an eye on that Apple memory story — it could be the catalyst that defines the week ahead.
